retrieved_docs.content: One major and one minor clinical criteria or the presence of three minor clinical criteria

retrieved_docs.title: Infectious Endocarditis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Subsequent bacteremia then allows for colonization of the vegetation. The necessary bacteremia can stem from an established, distant source of infection or emerge transiently secondary to intermittent hematogenous inoculation of oral flora from dental/gingival manipulation. Although the minimum bacterial burden remains unknown, experimental models have precipitated cases of infectious endocarditis with slow 1 mL infusions of 106 colony-forming units of bacteria. [9] Even in the setting of endocardial injury and bacteremia, pathogenesis still requires a virulent organism capable of binding to and facilitating platelet-fibrin deposits. For instance, three S. aureus proteins (clumping factors A, B, and serine-aspartate repeat protein) have been found to independently mediate platelet aggregation. In theory, expanding the originally sterile platelet-fibrin deposits protects pathogens from the host’s immune response and allows the vegetation to grow. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Endocarditis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the etiology of bacterial endocarditis. Review the presentation of a patient with bacterial endocarditis. Summarize the treatment and management options available for bacterial endocarditis. Explain the interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication regarding the management of patients with bacterial endocarditis. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Neurological Sequelae of Endocarditis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Valvular disease such as rheumatic heart disease and congenital heart abnormalities like a bicuspid aortic valve, ventricular septal defect, coarctation of the aorta are risk factors for infectious endocarditis. Pathogens that account for infectious endocarditis vary and depend on the risk factors including intravenous drug abuse, existing valvular dysfunction, poor oral hygiene, and dental procedure, immunocompromised status, the cardiac implantable electronic device (CIED), or previous history of infectious endocarditis. In infectious endocarditis, neurological complications correlate with the size of vegetation and bacterial load. Among all the causative pathogens, Staphylococci aureus is the most common microorganism of infective endocarditis in the United States and other developed countries. Staphylococci aureus IE is significantly more likely to result in embolic events than other pathogens. Neurological sequelae from IE are as following, Embolic stroke is caused by vegetation debris that lodge in the cerebral blood vessels and ended up resulting in infarction; Transient ischemic attack (TIA) is an episode of neurologic dysfunction attributed to ischemia of brain, spinal cord, or retina; Cerebral hemorrhage Meningitis Brain abscess Toxic encephalopathy Seizure

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Endocarditis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac murmurs are observed in about 85% of patients. Congestive heart failure develops in 30% to 40% of patients usually due to valvular dysfunction. Other signs include cutaneous manifestations such as petechiae or splinter hemorrhages (non-blanching linear reddish-brown lesions under the nail bed).

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Endocarditis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial endocarditis refers to infection of the endocardial surface of the heart. It usually involves heart valves, but it can occur on the endocardium or intracardiac devices. Acute endocarditis is a febrile illness that rapidly damages cardiac structures and spreads hematogenously which can progress to death within weeks if not treated. Subacute endocarditis has a slower disease process and may be present for weeks to months with gradual progression unless complicated by major embolic event or ruptured structure. This activity reviews the cause, pathophysiology, and presentation of bacterial endocarditis and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in its management.

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Endocarditis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial endocarditis refers to infection of the endocardial surface of the heart. It usually involves heart valves, but it can occur on the endocardium or intracardiac devices. There are two types: Acute endocarditis is a febrile illness that rapidly damages cardiac structures and spreads hematogenously which can progress to death within weeks if not treated. Subacute endocarditis has a slower disease process and may be present for weeks to months with gradual progression unless complicated by major embolic event or ruptured structure. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Endocarditis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Fever is the most common symptom. It can be associated with chills, night sweats, anorexia, weight loss, loss of appetite, malaise, headache, myalgias, arthralgias, abdominal pain, dyspnea, cough, and pleuritic pain.

retrieved_docs.title: Endocarditis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: In developed countries, congenital heart disease is the most important predisposing factor. In approximately 30% of patients with infective endocarditis, a predisposing factor is acknowledged and identified.

retrieved_docs.title: Subacute Bacterial Endocarditis Prophylaxis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify patients at risk for subacute bacterial endocarditis based on clinical history, underlying cardiac conditions, and predisposing factors. Implement appropriate antibiotic prophylaxis strategies in accordance with current guidelines and evidence-based recommendations for subacute bacterial endocarditis. Select optimal antibiotic regimens for prophylaxis based on patient-specific factors, including allergies, comorbidities, and procedural risks, for treating subacute bacterial endocarditis. Collaborate with interprofessional team members, including cardiologists and infectious disease specialists, to ensure comprehensive management of patients at risk for subacute bacterial endocarditis. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Subacute Bacterial Endocarditis Prophylaxis -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Infective endocarditis can manifest as either acute or subacute infection. [2] Acute infections are characterized by rapid progression, marked by high fevers, rigors, and sepsis. Conversely, the diagnosis of subacute bacterial endocarditis is often delayed and is characterized by nonspecific symptoms such as weight loss, fatigue, and dyspnea over a period of weeks to months. Clear distinctions exist between subacute and acute bacterial endocarditis. Penicillin-sensitive Streptococcus viridans is the predominant cause of most cases of subacute bacterial endocarditis, whereas Staphylococcus aureus is responsible for the majority of acute bacterial endocarditis cases. [3] Managing both types of bacteria necessitates distinct prophylactic approaches to prevent further complications of infective endocarditis.

retrieved_docs.title: Infectious Endocarditis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: History often reveals predisposing conditions and risk factors that aid with diagnosis. Current or previous indwelling catheterization, intravenous drug use, recent pacemaker placement, or history of prosthetic valves suggests predisposing endocardial injury. [5] The clinician should also inquire about known degenerative valve disease such as calcific aortic stenosis or mitral valve prolapse, which underlie about 30% of all cases. [3] [15] Previously a major risk factor for infectious endocarditis, rheumatic heart disease precedes the onset of less than 5% of infective endocarditis cases in the developed world today. In North America, diabetes mellitus represents one of the most common comorbidities.

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Endocarditis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: In developed countries, the incidence of endocarditis ranges from 2.6 to 7 cases per 100,000 population per year. The median age of patients with endocarditis is 58 years.

retrieved_docs.title: Infectious Endocarditis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Infectious endocarditis is the inflammation of the endocardium, the inner lining of the heart, as well as the valves that separate each of the four chambers within the heart. It is primarily a disease caused by bacteria and has a wide array of manifestations and sequelae. Without early identification and treatment, a myriad of intracardiac and far-reaching extracardiac complications can develop. Therefore, careful evaluation, including a thorough history and physical exam, can help diagnoses cases and guide management, limiting mortality and morbidity.

retrieved_docs.title: Endocarditis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In the era of interventional cardiology and radiology, infective endocarditis by Staphylococcus aureus is gaining more relevance. Nearly 30% of patients who develop infective endocarditis are dead within 12 months. Besides the infection damaging the valve, there is also a risk of embolization and stroke. The diagnosis and management of infective endocarditis are not always easy.

retrieved_docs.title: Endocarditis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: It is common to find infective endocarditis as a complication of congenital heart disease or rheumatic heart disease. However, is important to mention that infective endocarditis can present in children without any abnormal valves or cardiac malformations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2293
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic carditis, acute rheumatic pancarditis, acute rheumatic valvulitis, الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحاد (acute rheumatic pericarditis), and الْتِهابُ الشَّغافِ الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحادّ (acute rheumatic endocarditis) are all manifestations of acute rheumatic heart disease that share a common clinical course characterized by a sudden onset and/or short duration. This abrupt and brief presentation is a key feature across these related conditions, aiding clinicians in recognizing the rapid evolution of rheumatic involvement of the heart’s structures and informing timely diagnostic and therapeutic decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1782
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial myocarditis (التهاب عضل القلب الجرثومي) is an overarching infectious cardiac condition that encompasses pericarditis, endocarditis, myocarditis, and cardiomyopathy. These four entities represent inflammation of the pericardium, inflammation of the endocardium, inflammation of the myocardium, and disease of the heart muscle, respectively, and each can arise as a manifestation of bacterial infection of the heart. Understanding that pericarditis, endocarditis, myocarditis, and cardiomyopathy are subcategories of bacterial myocarditis aids clinicians in recognizing the full spectrum of bacterial cardiac involvement and in delivering comprehensive diagnostic and therapeutic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 186
retrieved_docs.content: bacterial form التهاب عضل القلب الجرثومي, both classified under peri‑, endo‑, and myocarditis and cardiomyopathy. Pericarditis is clinically associated with abnormal ECG findings. These interrelated classifications, clinical signs, and pharmacologic agents illustrate the hierarchical organization of acute coronary events, chronic arteriosclerotic processes, inflammatory cardiac disorders, and modern lipid‑lowering therapies, supporting precise diagnosis and targeted patient management in cardiovascular medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1449
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic pancarditis, acute rheumatic valvulitis, acute rheumatic myocarditis (التهاب عضل القلب الروماتزمي الحاد), acute rheumatic pericarditis (الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحاد), and acute rheumatic endocarditis (الْتِهابُ الشَّغافِ الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحادّ) all share a clinical course that is marked by sudden onset and/or short duration. This rapid and brief presentation is a common feature of the various acute rheumatic heart disease manifestations, indicating that clinicians should anticipate an abrupt, short‑lived disease course when evaluating and managing these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 231
retrieved_docs.content: replacement of the coronary valve (استبدال الصمام التاجي), and transcatheter implantation of the aortic valve—are grouped under heart valve procedures (عمليات صمام القلب) and share the cardiovascular system as their anatomical focus. Inflammatory and systemic cardiac conditions are integrated within the same framework: bacterial myocarditis (التهاب عضل القلب الجرثومي) represents a specific form of peri‑, endo‑, and myocarditis and cardiomyopathy; posterior diabetic network involvement (اعْتِلاَلُ الشَّبَكِيَّةِ السكري الخلفي) and central nervous system vasculitis (التهاب وعائي بالجهاز العصبي المركزي) illustrate the cardiovascular system’s relevance to broader systemic pathologies. The International Classification of Primary Care places primary‑care encounters related to these entities under the cardiovascular system and under “medication, treatment and therapeutic procedures,” underscoring the central role of cardiovascular assessment, monitoring, therapeutic intervention, and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2644
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic fever (الحُمَّى الروماتيزمية) is clinically associated with the need for aortic valve replacement (استبدال الصمام الأبهري) and coronary valve replacement (استبدال الصمام التاجي). The inflammatory damage caused by rheumatic fever can affect heart valves, leading to severe dysfunction of the aortic and coronary valves that may require surgical replacement. Recognizing this relationship enables clinicians to monitor patients with rheumatic fever for progressive valvular disease and to plan timely surgical intervention, thereby improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 233
retrieved_docs.content: is linked to loss of biological function (Failure) and may occur concurrently with the rapid cardiac inflammation of acute rheumatic conditions, underscoring the potential for extra‑cardiac organ dysfunction. Overall, the knowledge base depicts rheumatic fever and its cardiac sequelae as a tightly connected group of entities—ranging from valve‑specific failures (particularly mitral and tricuspid) to diffuse myocardial and pericardial inflammation—typically presenting abruptly and briefly, with possible simultaneous hepatic and renal compromise.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2780
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic fever (الحُمَّى الروماتيزمية) is identified as a cause of non‑hypertensive congestive heart failure (congestive heart failure; nonhypertensive). This form of congestive heart failure is classified as a subtype of rheumatic heart disease, other unspecified (مرض قلبي روماتزمي، غير ذلك وغير محدد). Consequently, rheumatic fever can lead to rheumatic heart disease, which may manifest clinically as non‑hypertensive congestive heart failure, highlighting the pathogenic link between the infectious trigger, the underlying cardiac disease category, and its specific functional presentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 178
retrieved_docs.content: insertion techniques across cardiac, neurological, and alternative‑medicine contexts. The cardiovascular system is also the anatomic site for disease entities such as posterior diabetic retinopathy, bacterial myocarditis, and central nervous system vasculitis. Bacterial myocarditis is further classified under peri‑, endo‑, and myocarditis and is considered a form of cardiomyopathy. Together, these relationships demonstrate how electrocardiographic monitoring, Holter technology, stress‑testing protocols, phonocardiography, invasive catheter‑based and surgical valve procedures, and disease classifications interconnect to support comprehensive cardiac evaluation, diagnosis, and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 1317
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic pancarditis, acute rheumatic valvulitis, التهاب عضل القلب الروماتزمي الحاد (acute rheumatic myocarditis), الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحاد (acute rheumatic pericarditis), and مرض قلبي روماتزمي حاد، غير محدد (unspecified acute rheumatic heart disease) are all manifestations of acute rheumatic cardiac involvement that share a common clinical course characterized by a sudden onset and/or short duration. This rapid and brief presentation is a key feature for clinicians to recognize when evaluating patients with rheumatic heart disease, as it helps differentiate these acute forms from more chronic rheumatic cardiac conditions and guides timely diagnostic and therapeutic decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2866
retrieved_docs.content: Congestive heart failure; nonhypertensive is identified as a manifestation of مرض قلبي روماتزمي مزمن (chronic rheumatic heart disease) and its development is attributed to الحُمَّى الروماتيزمية (rheumatic fever). This relationship underscores that rheumatic fever can lead to a non‑hypertensive form of congestive heart failure, which is classified within the spectrum of chronic rheumatic cardiac disease, informing both diagnostic considerations and therapeutic strategies.

Pericarditis (الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر) is clinically associated with abnormal ECG findings, highlighting the importance of electrocardiographic monitoring in inflammatory heart disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2028
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic carditis, acute rheumatic pancarditis, and acute rheumatic valvulitis are each described as having a clinical course characterized by sudden onset and/or short duration. This pattern of abrupt presentation and brief disease course is reflected in the Arabic terminology الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُور الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحاد, which denotes acute rheumatic inflammation, and مرض قلبي روماتزمي حاد، غير محدد, which refers to an unspecified acute rheumatic heart disease. Recognizing that these acute rheumatic manifestations share this rapid and limited clinical trajectory aids clinicians in timely diagnosis and appropriate management of rheumatic heart conditions.
